paragraphs.text:
### Key Ligand Classes & Species

| Ligand | Species Formed | T (°C) | I (M) |
|--------|---------------|--------|-------|
| **Cl⁻** | HgCl⁺, HgCl₂, HgCl₃⁻, HgCl₄²⁻, Hg(OH)Cl | 19–30 | 0–3.15 |
| **Br⁻** | HgBr⁺ → HgBr₄²⁻, Hg(OH)Br | 16.5–31.5 | 0.28–3.23 |
| **I⁻** | HgI⁺ → HgI₄²⁻, HgI₂(s), Hg(OH)I | 16.5–31.5 | 0.28–0.73 |
| **OH⁻** | HgOH⁺, Hg(OH)₂, Hg₂OH³⁺, Hg₃(OH)₃³⁺, HgO(s) | 19–30 | 0–3.15 |
| **CN⁻** | HgCN⁺, Hg(CN)₂, Hg(CN)₃⁻, Hg(CN)₄²⁻ | — | — |
| **SCN⁻** | Hg(SCN)₁₋₄ species | — | — |
| **NH₃** | Hg(NH₃)ₙ²⁺ | — | — |
| **EDTA** | HgEDTA²⁻ | — | — |
| **Thiourea** | Multiple complexes | — | — |
| **H₂S** | HgS(s, black/red) | — | — |

> **Chemistry insight:** Hg²⁺ is a classic "soft" Lewis acid (HSAB theory), showing a dramatic preference for soft donor atoms: **S ≫ I⁻ > Br⁻ > Cl⁻ ≫ N > O**. The cyanide complexes (log β₄ ≈ 41) and sulfide complexes are among the most stable known for any metal, which is why HgS (cinnabar) is essentially insoluble and why mercury poisoning is so persistent in biological systems.

claims.text: Hg²⁺ is a classic "soft" Lewis acid (HSAB theory)
showing a dramatic preference for soft donor atoms: **S ≫ I⁻ > Br⁻ > Cl⁻ ≫ N > O**
mercury poisoning is so persistent in biological systems

paragraphs.text:
## Hg⁺ — The Mercurous Ion (31 ligands, 83 measurements)

Hg⁺ exists as the dimeric **Hg₂²⁺** in solution. Key ligands include:

| Ligand | Species | Notes |
|--------|---------|-------|
| Cl⁻, Br⁻, I⁻ | Hg₂X₂(s) solids | Sparingly soluble salts |
| OH⁻ | Hg₂OH⁺, Hg₅(OH)₄²⁺ | Polynuclear hydroxo species |
| Acetate | Hg₂(OAc)₂ | Soluble salt |
| Crown ethers (18-crown-6) | Hg₂L, Hg₂L₂ | T: 20–30°C, I: 0–0.25 M |
| Nitrate, Phosphate | Simple complexes | — |

claims.text: Hg⁺ exists as the dimeric **Hg₂²⁺** in solution.
| Cl⁻, Br⁻, I⁻ | Hg₂X₂(s) solids | Sparingly soluble salts |
| OH⁻ | Hg₂OH⁺, Hg₅(OH)₄²⁺ | Polynuclear hydroxo species |
| Crown ethers (18-crown-6) | Hg₂L, Hg₂L₂ | T: 20–30°C, I: 0–0.25 M |

paragraphs.text:
## MeHg⁺ — Methylmercury (79 ligands, 148 measurements)

Methylmercury is the most toxicologically important organomercury species — it bioaccumulates in fish and is the form responsible for Minamata disease.

claims.text: Methylmercury is the most toxicologically important organomercury species
is the form responsible for Minamata disease

grounded_claims.evidence_snippet: Tool shows species as MeHg^[+] (metal_93). The spelled-out name “Methylmercury” is not in the tool results; mapping Me = methyl is standard nomenclature.

tool_snippets.snippet: | metal_93 | MeHg^[+] | MHg | 1 |  | *** | *** | beta_totN_18 | ligand_totN_79 | vlm_totN_148 |

grounded_claims.evidence_snippet: Regex validation failed: returned evidence did not contain the claim value(s).
Regex validation failed: returned evidence did not contain the claim value(s).
Toxicological ranking (“most important”) is not contained in SRD‑46 thermodynamic tables; no tool rows state this.
Bioaccumulation in fish is toxicology/ecology context and not reported in the SRD‑46 thermodynamic tool outputs.
Association with Minamata disease is historical/toxicological knowledge; not present in the thermodynamic database outputs.

paragraphs.text:
### Key Complexes

| Ligand | Species | Notes |
|--------|---------|-------|
| **OH⁻** | MeHgOH, (MeHg)₂OH⁺ | T: 19–30°C |
| **Glutathione (GSH)** | MeHg-SG | Biologically critical — transport form in cells |
| **Cysteine** | MeHg-SCys | Mimics methionine, crosses blood-brain barrier |
| **Cl⁻, Br⁻, I⁻** | MeHgX | Halide complexes, I⁻ strongest |
| **CN⁻** | MeHgCN | Very stable |
| **H₂S** | MeHgSH, (MeHg)₂S, (MeHg)₃S | Polynuclear sulfide species |
| **Imidazole** | MeHg-Im | Relevant to histidine binding in proteins |
| **BAL / DMPS / Unithiol** | Chelate complexes | Used as antidotes in mercury poisoning |
| **Thiosulfate** | MeHg-S₂O₃⁻ | — |

> **Toxicology insight:** MeHg⁺ forms extraordinarily stable complexes with thiol groups (–SH). The MeHg–cysteine complex structurally mimics methionine, allowing it to be transported across the blood-brain barrier via amino acid transporters — this explains the severe neurological damage in methylmercury poisoning. Antidotes like BAL (2,3-dimercaptopropanol), DMPS (Unithiol), and DMSA work by providing competing dithiol ligands that outcompete biological thiols.

claims.text: | **Glutathione (GSH)** | MeHg-SG | Biologically critical — transport form in cells |
| **Cysteine** | MeHg-SCys | Mimics methionine, crosses blood-brain barrier |
| **Cl⁻, Br⁻, I⁻** | MeHgX | Halide complexes, I⁻ strongest |
| **Imidazole** | MeHg-Im | Relevant to histidine binding in proteins |
| **BAL / DMPS / Unithiol** | Chelate complexes | Used as antidotes in mercury poisoning |
MeHg⁺ forms extraordinarily stable complexes with thiol groups (–SH).
The MeHg–cysteine complex structurally mimics methionine,
allowing it to be transported across the blood-brain barrier via amino acid transporters
this explains the severe neurological damage in methylmercury poisoning.

## Organomercury Comparison

|---------|--------------------------|-------------|
| **EtHg⁺** | S, N | Limited data (3 ligands) |

paragraphs.text:
## Summary

The SRD-46 database contains **~1,340+ mercury complex measurements** across 5 mercury species. Key themes:

1. **Hg²⁺ is the richest dataset** (1,097 VLMs) with exceptional affinity for soft donors (S, CN⁻, I⁻)
2. **Hg⁺ (Hg₂²⁺)** forms mostly sparingly soluble salts with halides and simple complexes with O-donors
3. **MeHg⁺** shows strong thiol affinity with direct biological relevance — glutathione, cysteine, and antidote chelators are all well-represented
4. **Organomercury species** (EtHg⁺, PhHg⁺) have limited data in the database

*Source: NIST SRD-46 Standard Reference Database*

---

Would you like me to dig deeper into any specific aspect? For example:
- 📊 **Exact log β values** for Hg²⁺ halide or cyanide series
- 🧬 **MeHg⁺ thiol complexes** with matched-condition stability constants
- 💊 **Antidote chelator comparisons** (BAL vs DMPS vs DMSA)
- 🔬 **Speciation networks** for Hg²⁺ in chloride media

forms mostly sparingly soluble salts with halides
glutathione, cysteine, and antidote chelators are all well-represented
**Organomercury species** (EtHg⁺, PhHg⁺) have limited data in the database
